Well hello everyone this is Bill Glazer bringing you another one of our Gold Plus monthly calls and we got a really interesting great call for it today actually Dan and I are both looking forward to this call because whenever we talk to Joe Vitale we always enjoy that conversation always learn a bunch of stuff ourselves and Joe how you doing? Doing great, got a beautiful day out here in Texas, sun's out, sky's clear, I want to go for a drive after this it looks great. For those people that are not familiar with who you are and now we only have an hour and a half to tell your story Joe. Can you give everybody sort of like a brief bio and and don't forget to mention sort of your new found additional fame with the secret and and I probably ask you a couple questions even about that today but but everybody know a little bit about you. All right well I tell people I'm primarily an author and I've written so many books now that my back of the business card says author of way too many books to list here. I am a internet marketer, my main website Mr. Fire.com I've been published in every way you can think of from ebooks, the traditional books, self-published books, print on demand books, audio books, I have a program with Nightingale Conant called the power of outrageous marketing. I am in the movie The Secret which is making me a little bit of a mini celebrity. I ended up on Larry King. All of this has helped my career, helped my book sales, helped the traffic to my website. It's important to realize that it didn't all start this way. I was homeless some 30 some years ago in the Dallas area, migrated to Houston where I struggled in virtual poverty for a long time and slowly pecked away at my career. My first book was probably in 1983 or so then in the art of writing. It's now a collector's item. If you find it on eBay you'll probably pay top dollar for it. At this point the sky is the limit. I am looking skyward. I got people who want to turn my books into movies. I've got other companies that want to have me have my own weekly TV show. I'm enjoying a pretty wild ride at this point. You took your 34 years to become an overnight success. I tell people, yeah, I just wanted to take you to become an overnight success. Generally about 27 years. That's what I keep hearing. Two decades or more. Well, I guess, first I want to talk about today's topic is how PT Bartum would have done it. You just recently wrote a book. What is the exact title of your new book? The one I think you're referring to is on Barnum. It's called There's a Customer Born Every Minute. Yeah, I think it's one of the best marketing books ever. It will tend to motivate people to go back and do some other reading about Barnum as well. I had been through it and borrowed from it and used it and then set it aside. I got back into it because Joe's speaking for Rory Faddett at his boot camp this year. On how PT Bartum would promote your restaurant. I not only reread Joe's book, but I reread four or five books about Barnum. The advertising and marketing and promotion lessons would stretch from here to Mars. Joe did a spectacular job of synthesizing it in that book. I guess, although I got most of our questions are for Joe today, but I guess, Dan, I'll go a little bit out of the ordinary today. Can you start off with sharing maybe one or two or three of the big lessons that you sort of recall that you took away from the book? Well, yeah, the biggest thing is, however it is that you want to say it, you've got to be focused on creating events that add to your fame. It doesn't really matter whether we're talking about a small business owner in his community or somebody operating in a bigger way, in a bigger arena. The message of Barnum's life from the rather disreputable circus promoter to the more reputable museum owner, that ultimately, ironically, a speaker in seminar presenter on success like the rest of us, is that we're all in the self-promotion business. I think a lot of business owners miss that point. They're so focused on their product or their service or their business that they neglect the most obvious and most controllable point of distinction and differentiation, which is them. Whether promoting themselves or their businesses has Joe point out in a number of ways in the book. I mean, Barnum was about constantly creating the next big thing, the next big event that everybody would be talking about and the media would be paying attention to and the customers would flock to see and then you need another one and then you need another one and then you need another one. It's interesting to me that some of my clients complain about this and very recently in two different consulting days, I've had them whining about the need to keep coming up with the next big thing. But in reality, that I think is the business that we're all in and I would imagine Joe would agree and is seeing the payoff from the latest of a series of big things that he has created, the Barnum thing being one of those. Absolutely. It's too bad that people are so lazy and they're so fearful about taking any action. I am constantly putting on events. I hired a famous hoaxer to put on what has become the greatest lotto humbug in the history of our country and that was a year ago February. Alan Abel, who was a living PT Barnum at this point in his 80s, some people may know him, Daniel may know of him or read his books. He's the guy who started a movement to clothe animals. Yeah, I'll say so. And it was a joke. It was a statement against censorship, but so many people bought it as a reality that it lasted for five years and fooled everybody, including Walter Cronkite, who still mad at Alan Abel for pulling that stunt. And Alan Abel also pulled an event where he created taxpayers anonymous, which was a movement to audit the IRS. And Alan said he wanted all of the US government's canceled checks brought to his house. And all of these were promotional events that brought a lot of attention to Alan Abel and anything he was involved with. I went to Alan when I found out he was still alive and I said, I want to pull a great stunt to promote one of my products. And at that point, I wanted to promote my book, The Attractor Factor. And he thought about it and he came back a few weeks later with this master plan, this master strategy for somebody to win the great Powerball Lotto. He had to wait until it got up to, I think it went to $365 million, which is the largest amount ever at that point, and maybe ever to this point. And he arranged for somebody to pretend to win the Lotto, win that Lotto numbers were announced. And that person was interviewed by the media. It was on Good Morning America. And every time the person was interviewed, they asked him, where did you get the numbers? Where did you get the winning numbers? And he said, well, I was just flipping through this book, The Attractor Factor. So it was bringing a lot of attention to me. We filmed all of this. And of course, a few days after the hoax, it was revealed that it was a humbug. But I got a lot of mileage out of it. My book, my name, were all in newspapers. I still remember when a reporter called me and said, I understand you were the person who financed this Lotto hoax. And I was a little nervous about it because I thought he was going to tear me a new one. And I said, yeah, I was the guy who did it. He said, why did you do it? And in that moment, divine intervention came in and I lived. It was easier to fake winning the Lotto than it was to actually win it. He laughed and it ended up being on the front page of one of the nation's newspapers. And of course, we also filmed everything. And that is going to be released in a week or so as a documentary called Humbug, the story of the greatest Lotto hoax in history. So that's only one of many. I'm always looking for new ones. I just contacted Alan Abel again, and I've talked to other people, Paul Hartunian, many of the others you would know who know about publicity and the power of a media event because I want to keep these happening. And why do I want to keep these happening? For the same reason, P.T. Barnum did it. He needed to keep his name on the surface of everybody's mind. And he needed to keep doing new events to keep people coming back to his place of business. This is just the principle that works in business and not enough people even think of it, let alone do it. Yeah, Joe, just for a point of reference, when did you sort of first discover and become embroiled with P.T. Barnum? Yeah, a great question. Probably 20 years ago, I read a book by Aug Mandino called The University of Success. And it was a collection of excerpts from Success Literature. And in it was a chapter from P.T. Barnum's autobiography. And I read it and I was dazzled by it. I thought, this is a humorous guy. This is a creative man. This is an audacious man. I then went for his autobiography, which is easy to find. P.T. Barnum was so brilliant. He put his autobiography in the public domain while he was alive, meaning that anybody could publish it and Barnum didn't have to pay for the printing cost. Anybody could promote it and profit from it. And Barnum didn't care about the money about it because he knew he was getting his word, his name, he was getting the free publicity. So I found a copy of his autobiography. I read it. I marveled at this. This man was creative, audacious. He was a genius at marketing, a genius at advertising. And one thing that I absolutely was enthralled by was the idea that he saw something in a person-placer thing that others didn't see. They took it for granted. My favorite story is when probably around 1845, P.T. Barnum was introduced to a little boy in Connecticut who did not grow. And everybody looked at the little boy and said, isn't it sad that he's never going to be taller than three feet tall? The little boy's name was Charlie Stratton. And everybody felt sentimental and emotional about him. But Barnum looked at him and fought Superstar. He renamed them General Tom Thumb. And he taught that little boy to sing and dance. And he took that little boy who had died unknown and probably penniless and made him into one of our first superstars and made him incredibly wealthy. And little Tom Thumb grew to the age of 40. He died, I think, from a stroke at 41. He had sailboats. He had mansions. He had traveled the world. He had entertained kings and queens and royalty. He was beloved. He was probably America's first superstar, not the world's first superstar. So my introduction to Barnum was through his autobiography, which I still love reading. I still smile and laugh about when I think of some of the stories in it. And you have also, I mean, very closely had now associated yourself with him. I know... I almost went into business channeling PT Barnum's business advice to people. It was a joke. I thought, you know what? Barnum would probably do this. He was a magician. He was a hypnotist. He was a debunker of spiritualism. But I think that he would have gone along with a hoax if he could have channeled some dead business person from before his time. I think he'd admire the fact that if I took out ads on the internet or something or gave out business cards saying, how would PT Barnum improve your business today for $1,000 an hour? I will channel the spirit of Barnum through me. So I haven't done that one yet. By the way, it could be a great gig. One of our members, Dave Dee, who's a magician. David, his next bootcamp is channeling Andrew Carnage. I heard about that. That's great. I love it. I love it. Barnum would have loved it. Well, you know, one of the things about attaching yourself to Barnum, I mean, that's a Barnum principle as well. We just actually talked about it at advanced consulting and coaching bootcamp. I just finished doing it. The principle of attachment, of hooking yourself to somebody who is better known or perhaps more promotional in one way or another than yourself. Barnum did it in order to strive for some fresh level of respectability with the opera singer. I forget. Jenny Lin. Jenny Lin. That's right. He heard about this famous European opera singer who was known to have a voice like a nightingale. And he wanted to earn some respectability. Though he was pretty bold and fearless about everything that he did, he did want people to think of him as putting on something of a more cultural event rather than sideshow type events. So he arranged for Jenny Lin to come to America and he financed that and mortgaged himself to bring her over. And to his amazement, he realized one day when he was coming out of a hotel and the person opened the door for him, the dormant there. And P.T. Barnum said, I'm bringing Jenny Lin to America. And the dormant said, who is that? And Barnum realized with a shutter that nobody in America knew who she was. She was famous in Europe. And he had his work cut out for him. But he hired publicist. He created events. He focused on her generosity that she gave a lot of her money to people. This is another touchstone in getting the masses to pay attention. He didn't focus so much on her singing abilities. He focused a lot on her humanitarian efforts. By the time he was done with his publicity and Jenny Lin arrived at Doc in New York, 30,000 people met her there. And these were 30,000 people who did not know who she was until P.T. Barnum started his publicity efforts. So he knew the value of attaching himself to a name. But he also knew, in many cases, he's got to promote who the hell that person is for them to respect the person and him. I've done something similar. I like telling the story because it illustrates a few Barnum-like principles. And that's the idea of, I was selling a package in 2004 that sold for something like $1,000. It was called Hypnotic Selling Secrets. And I was looking for all kinds of ways to promote it. And I was promoting it heavily online. But I thought there's a whole world out there. P.T. Barnum realized that there was truly a customer born every minute. He didn't just focus on one way of reaching people. He did everything he could think of and everything technologically available to do it. And I thought, well, Barnum was alive today. He wouldn't want me to just be on the internet. And I was watching TV one night and I saw on a commercial, Brittany Spears had a new commercial come out. And it was for some cologne she was endorsing. And I looked at it and it was fairly mesmerizing. And then the spirit of P.T. Barnum pulled the flashlight or the light bulb over my head and I realized there's a news release here. And I went to my computer and I wrote a news release that said, Brittany Spears accused of using Hypnotic Selling Secrets in her new TV ad. I was associating myself with a celebrity. This was very legitimate because I had a Hypnotic marketer, Hypnotic writer, Hypnotic salesperson, had a whole package on Hypnotic Selling Secrets. So I could comment on her marketing efforts and I sent that out as a news release. I got tens of thousands of new people that were not just on the internet. They were on the internet, off the internet, all over the place who suddenly were aware of me, my product, my service and everything I was doing. When I did my next tele-seminar, which was like a week or so after that news release about Brittany Spears, we had record breaking numbers. They were like, I think if I remember right, there were 13,000 people who came on a tele-seminar to hear me talk about Hypnotic Selling Secrets and how Brittany Spears was using them and misusing them. 13,000 people went typically we had 500 people on a tele-seminar. So this was publicity. This was thinking in a bigger way. This is what Barnum would do. And in this case, I was taking a ride off of the fame of Brittany Spears. People searching for her name would suddenly see my name associated with hers and a whole new audience became aware of me. So obviously, one of the things that we want our members who are listening to this call to be able to connect to Dotson is that the same principles that BT Barnum always taught in order to make himself famous and to continue to come up with the next thing can translate to any kind of business, any kind of industry. You're speaking for Roy Fadett as restaurant, marketing system bootcamp, which obviously you'll be talking there about that. And talk a little bit more about that. I mean, somebody who's listening to this right now, I mean, we have, as you know, Joe, we have an extremely diverse types of niches and industries that are members of ours. Probably today there's not a niche or an industry that's not a member of ours. How would you recommend to people to sort of use a PT Barnum in their business? How should they think in order to make that happen? Well, the first thing I want to say is that they don't have to be outrageous in the way a lot of people think outrageous. That scares a lot of people in business. They don't want to ride anywhere on an elephant. They don't want to jump out of a plane. They don't want to hang upside down like Houdini in a straight jacket. All of these things work. All of these things are attention-getting, but I want to comfort the little nervous soul that are out there who says I'm an accountant or I have a dry cleaning store or I'm in business doing auto mechanics and I don't want to do something outrageous or outlandish. So you don't have to, but you do have to think a little bit more daringly and a little bit more creatively. For example, one of Barnum's techniques for getting famous was simply writing. And that's one of my favorite ones I know Dan would relate to this. I am first and foremost an author. I love writing books and by writing books, writing articles, writing sales letters, writing news releases, it was one of the ways that I began to get a degree of fame for myself. As long as I wrote how-to information, tip sheets, secrets for doing this or for doing that, and people liked them and they kept reading them. They passed them to others, so there was free publicity. There was viral marketing going on. Very often they got printed in magazines. Very often I can just collect them and turn them into a book. When I did come out with books, of course, as long as I market them, a whole new audience hears of me, hears of my business. I don't do very much consulting these days. I don't write very many sales letters for other people these days. I'm too busy on the speaking circuit and getting royalties for this, that, and the other. But in the early days, when I was first starting out, like back in Houston, as I wrote articles, sometimes for free, like DBA Magazine, which was the business magazine in the 80s and 90s in Houston, I would write the marketing article in there. I would write a column called Marketing Matters. People would read it and call me up, eager to hire me. Why did they want to hire me? I wasn't much better, much different. I wasn't in any way more gifted than anybody else that was doing marketing or copywriting. Because they read the article, they felt like I knew what I was talking about. So one of the easiest takeaway principles here that Barnum loved doing was simply writing, writing an article, writing a tip sheet, writing a news release. Of course, Barnum wrote many books. He didn't just write his autobiography. One of my most cherished volumes that was the hardest to find was A Collection of Jokes, Funny Stories by P.T. Barnum. It cost me quite a bit to get, but it's one of my favorite books. So, and jokes, by the way, while we're thinking of this, jokes in any particular business can become collectible. I'd love to see somebody in the, an accountant collect all the favorite tax jokes of all time. These things are something that are popular, that people pass around to each other. They'd be willing to buy. It brings attention to the business. It's probably even worth a news release once you've done it. So one of the easiest things to do is writing. Another thing that P.T. Barnum did, that anybody in business, no matter what the business happens to be, can do, and that's speaking. I found in the early days, for me, when I spoke to business groups, networking groups, rotary clubs, anybody that was going to take me, and I had a topic, and this again is a key point. I had a topic they wanted to hear about. They didn't want to hear Joe's life story. They wanted to know how I can solve a business problem or how I can increase their business or how I can help them achieve some outcome, some particular result. As long as I talked about that, they would hire me at the end of that talk or they would buy whatever it is that I was talking, and they would invite me, somebody in that audience, had another group and would invite me to go to that group and speak. Barnum did this. At one point in his life, he went bankrupt, and he only went bankrupt because he loved Bridgeport, Connecticut, so much. He was mayor of that city twice, and he had pledged for a company, the Jerome Clark Company, to move all of their headquarters to Bridgeport and bring business to Bridgeport and to give jobs to people in Bridgeport, and the company ripped him off. He went bankrupt. One of the things he did to regain his fortune and become a multimillionaire again is he went on the speaking circuit, and he gave a talk called, I already forgot what it is, The Art of Making Money. The Art of Making Money. The Art of Getting Money. The Art of Getting Money. Thank you, then. The Art of Getting Money. And he was reluctant to give the talk, because he was at that point bankrupt, and he said, who wants to come here, a bankrupt man, and talk about the Art of Getting Money? And everybody pointed out, but you did make a fortune, and you know how to make money. Go give this talk. Tell your story. Give it some principles. At that point, charge 50 cents ahead in the 1850s, 1860s. He regained his fortune. So, once again, this is something that anybody in any business can do. They don't have to act crazy. They don't have to dress up in a costume. They don't have to ride a goat around the parking lot, but they can have fun and be in demand. No, there's anything wrong with the goat around the parking lot. No, there is nothing wrong with that. I want to point out one of the things I did last year, and again, this was to promote PT Barnum and a book I was writing on PT Barnum. I held the world's first canine concert. And a canine concert is where a rock band plays music that's at a sound level only dogs can hear. And the whole thing was an elaborate joke. It was a big humbug. On the stage, we did have a rock band, and we had all kind of fancy equipment that was coming from the speakers that made it look like the music they were playing was being funneled through the speakers and then diagrams and electrical this and that would show that it was being translated into a sound frequency that the humans there couldn't hear, but the dogs could hear. And on the stage was a six-foot copy of my book. There's a customer born every minute. And it was a magic trick. Out of that book, I produced PT Barnum, I produced a living gorgeous mermaid, and I produced the MC. The other reason for the big book is, no matter who was there, and even if you were two blocks away, you'd be able to look at that stage and you would see the cover of my book. When the media is there taking film, taking photos, my book cover was in all of that. So that's kind of silly. That's kind of audacious. That's kind of bold. But at the same time, we had four news crews there and it got national coverage, and we filmed the whole thing, and it's becoming another product called King 9 Concert, which is a documentary on that humbug. Once again, to sort of connect the dots for a lot of people, another principle that Dan teaches in the Renegade Millionaire System is that he has observed that Renegade Millionaires have a terrific ability to take ordinary businesses and turn them into extraordinary businesses, which is clearly the exact same thinking that Pt Barnum has. And here's my question is a little different. Not specific to Pt Barnum now, but you've been really teaching, I mean, as long as I've known you, you've been teaching to use Pt Barnum type of thinking and marketing and strategies for people to use in their ordinary businesses. And I'm putting you on the spot here now, but I don't think you're very capable of handling anything I could throw at you anyway. But we'll see. But give us an example or two of some ordinary businesses that you've worked with or sort of you've heard about the people that have read your books and then they've reported back to you how some people have taken ordinary businesses and made them extraordinary by using the Barnum type of thinking. Okay, well, the two examples that come to mind real quick, and I can go with one of them. We'll see if I remember the other one after I finish the first. One is there was a couple guys who had bought my program with Nightingale Connick called The Power of Outrageous Marketing, which does verbally express a lot of the things that are in the book. There's a customer born every minute. And they were thinking about this whole idea of turning average things into outrageous things, average things in the publicity magnets. And they began by being skeptical. It's like, is this really work? And then they started thinking, well, let's try it on something. Let's take something that's just plain boring, just plain crazy, just plain nothing and try to sell it. And they decided to focus on toilet paper. So they know toilet paper can sell, but you know, it doesn't sell that well unless you've eaten badly and you really need the food here in some different country. So they thought, what can we do with toilet paper to make this extraordinary, to make this valuable, to make it marketable? And this was during the time of the dot com that everybody was losing money and they were being disillusioned. So they decided to print on the toilet paper, falling stock prices for dot com companies. And they sent out a news release, which of course is something PT Barnum would easily do on his depth bed. PT Barnum said he owed most of the wealth of his empire to the newspapers of the world because he would send news releases out and they would run the stories for him, which would bring all of the traffic, the stampede, the money, the wealth, everything right to his door. The media did that. So these folks were now friends of mine. That's why I know the story. They told me this firsthand, made these toilet paper and they called it ticker tape toilet paper, ticker tape toilet paper. And they sent out a news release with the Wall Street Journal picked up and a bunch of other sources picked up in the Wall Street Journal alone. Just a single mention of ticker tape toilet paper caused them to get thousands of people going to their website where it was described. They never intended to sell the toilet paper. This was a test to see if the principle of taking something lame and making it more appetizing to the world could actually work. But as a result, they had made a price tag, which was fabricated of $10 a roll. Companies were buying it by the cartons. People were buying it and sending it to other people. People were buying it as a joke. People were buying it in companies and distributing it. They made a lot of money and got a lot of traffic from an experiment to turn an average nothing product into something that was more valuable. $10 a roll for toilet paper. Why? Because they printed something on it. The other story that comes to mind, I've been doing some research about the restaurants who are doing outlandish things. Partly so, I'm speaking at Rory's event. I want to make sure I do a great job for them. So I'm looking around to find, well, our restaurants really doing PT Barnum like things. Most of them aren't, but some of them are. One of them, but I thought was really a clever... It's an Indian restaurant, but instead of just saying Indian restaurant, come and have our great food, they say, we are in our Uvedic restaurant. You don't get to order here. You walk in and an are you Vedic medicine doctor? And this is, I don't know how to explain the are you Vedic medicine? It's an Indian approach to wellness. And then in this particular restaurant, you walk in the door and an are you Vedic practitioner slash waiter waitress sizes you up and tells you what you should eat that day for your best health. And I thought, now this is an interesting twist. It's a restaurant where you don't get to order. They tell you what you need to eat in order to feel better. And of course, there's other restaurants. There's one restaurant. I'm sure Paul Hartoony has talked about it and probably you, Dan, the one who made the, what was it, a 12 pound hamburger or a 14 pound hamburger? Just some sort of preposterous thing. And of course, it was done for outrageous mess. Again, just a restaurant. But what can we do to bring the unusual here or do the unusual? The media picks it up and even more amazing. People actually order it and take pictures of that, send it out. And those are a couple of examples that come to mind. Let me share one with you about one of, you know, as you know, Joe, my background is retail and I have a marketing system called BGS marketing, which originally sold to menswear retailers. And I have this one retailer who was in his name is it's called Strauss menswear. They're in Fargo, North Dakota. And Fargo, North Dakota, this time of year averages about minus 37 degrees. And they have every this time of year, they have a outdoor sidewalk sale minus 37 degrees. Now, most of the, most of the sales and transactions, as you can imagine, happen inside the store. But to advertise the sale, the owner's name is John Strauss. His father was the founder. His father now is in his early 90s. And to advertise the sale, they take a photo of his father standing outside of the store minus 37 degrees, wearing a pair of shorts and a parka to let people know about the outdoor and indoor sidewalk sale, Fargo, North Dakota has unbelievable media attention, you know, on this whole thing, you know, how can this retailer run an outdoor sidewalk sale in Fargo, North Dakota? Just the amount of media that they've garnered from this. More. I mean, they really don't even need to advertise the sale. You know, I need that trigger something that's worth talking about. And I don't hear enough marketing people talk about it. And that's the idea of an unusual graphic. The media loves visuals. They love pictures. They love video. They love anything that's got a visual there that shows something unusual. Barnum was known for this. He didn't like using photographs because he couldn't doctor photographs. He would use artwork. And even though Matthew Brady took some pictures of Barnum and some of his sideshow people and some of his museum, it was actually Barnum who liked using artwork or pictures that showed dramatic contrast. He might show a picture of Tom Fum, but if Tom Fum was just standing by himself with no table chair or anybody else around him, you didn't get a sense of his size. But if Tom Fum stood beside a Bobby in London, who was six or seven feet tall, you got a real sense that this guy is really small. So I like to call these hypnotic graphics. And I've seen everybody from Joe Cosman with his potato gun and some of the other stuff, the spud gun, and some of the other people out there who will illustrate a news release, a product, or even in an ad with something that's unusual, something that has contrast to it. It's not what you expect to see. And what you just described with the father standing out there in shorts and the freezing weather, there's contrast in there. This is a hypnotic image. It's something that everybody wants to see. When you send it to the media and they recognize it as, oh, this has got to be a grabber. Our readers will want to see this. They run it for you. This is another way of turning what could be the average or the, I don't know what the word is, but something that's not standing out and make it stand out with just an unusual picture.
